thailand.politicalContext: Thailand is the only SE Asian kingdom to avoid overt colonialism. A sometime democracy and oftentimes military-led state, it coalesces around the memory of 19th-century kings and the still-revered royal family. The Thai state uses Buddhism as a tool of state building, reinforcing Bangkok's hegemony. National history is shaped by Buddhism, royalism, and Thai parochialism. The Shan (Tai) are an ethnic minority in northwestern Thailand whose children are taught Central Thai in schools as cultural assimilation. Thailand's reformed monastic tradition limits full ordination (upasamapada) to males, effectively barring transgender persons (katoey) from bhikkhu ordination. The Thai Sangha operates under the Thammayut Nikaya and Mahanikai frameworks with formal hierarchies.
thailand.buddhistInstitutions: The village monastery (wat) is the center of community life, containing a preaching hall, pagoda, ordination hall, dwellings for monks, novices, and mae khaao (Buddhist nuns). Temple population fluctuates with at least one monk in residence. Mae khaao are local women living in simpler dwellings on monastery grounds. Khon naun tsaung (temple sleepers) are devout lay elders who commit to following more precepts and stay at the temple regularly. Religious services follow the lunar calendar on wan sin (precept days). Lay readers perform scriptural readings at merit-making ceremonies. The Sangha is supported materially by the lay community who provide food (last meal at 11:00 AM), shelter, and offerings.
keyHistoricalMoments: King Ram Khamhaeng of Sukhothai (d. 1298) was a fervent Buddhist king who honored both Buddha and the local spirit Phra Khaphung at his mandala capital
King Tilokaraja of Chiang Mai (1455) built a temple imitating Bodh Gaya and hosted a major Buddhist editorial assembly at Seven Spires Monastery
The Chakri dynasty (from 1782) established the line of Rama kings
1976: all-weather road built connecting Baan Kaung Mu village to Mae Hong Son, triggering modernization
Shan migration from Burma into Thailand accelerating over 100+ years due to political troubles in Burma
Cash economy increasingly penetrating subsistence agriculture; significant discretionary income goes to Buddhist rituals and merit-making
Early 20th-century sangha reforms created rigid gender binaries in monastic life
Forest ordination (tying monastic robes to trees) originated in northern Thailand in the late 1980s
distinctivePractices: Shan practice Theravada Buddhism combined with spirit beliefs involving phi (spirits) and the tsao moeng (village guardian spirit)
Traditional healing rituals combining Buddhist scripture, spirit exorcism (pat phi), and soul-calling (haung khwan)
Kotsaa: annual merit-making ceremony in the 11th lunar month for all who died in the previous year
Gambling at funerals and kotsaa ceremonies is a longstanding tradition
The tsao moeng shrine at village edge serves as source of healing power and community protection
Drinking the water of the tsao moeng -- a sobriety oath enforced by spiritual consequences
Temple sleeping: old people commit to staying at the temple on wan sin, keeping eight precepts, meditating, and progressively withdrawing from worldly life
Meditation with mak nap (Buddhist rosary beads) using popped rice as markers, repeating 'anicca, dukkha, anatta'
Kaentau (Paying Respect ceremony): biannual village-wide ritual where young people visit temple sleepers with flower bouquets and gifts
First-month bathing ceremony for newborns where temple sleepers tie white strings around wrists while uttering blessings
Wedding blessings where temple sleepers tie white strings around wrists of bride and groom
Khun Mae Tritrinn's Dantham nunnery in Chiang Dao represents 'third-way' monasticism for transgender practitioners, using Facebook (429,000 followers) for dhamma teaching
Mano-mayiddhi meditation (mind-made magical prowess) practiced at Dantham from the lineage of Luang Phor Ruesi Ling Dam
contemporaryIssues: Transgender persons (katoey) are barred from full bhikkhu ordination and perceived as 'Other' transgressing traditional gender boundaries
Katoey seeking monastic life face limited options: suppress identity for bhikkhu ordination, accept subordinate mae chii status, or seek alternative spaces like Dantham
Mainstream view that katoey femininity results from bad karma, though stopping ordination is also believed to cause bad karma
Gay monks generally accepted in monasteries and assigned clerical/nurturing roles, but katoey face active discouragement
Cash economy penetrating subsistence agriculture, changing the economics of merit-making
Forest ordination as environmental practice -- trees wrapped in robes considered sacred monks not to be destroyed
Buddhadasa Bhikkhu's influence: 'Preserving ecology means conserving dhamma as dhamma is our inner ecology'

myanmar.politicalContext: Once a proud kingdom, Burma was defeated by British armies in the 19th century and subsumed within the British Empire. Independence in 1948 brought dysfunctional democracy, then Ne Win's military rule wedding Buddhism and socialism. By the 1980s, Burma was backward, isolated, and riven by armed rebellions. Aung San Suu Kyi's democracy movement ended isolation, but the nation remains bitterly divided with hundreds of thousands of minorities fleeing. The military seized power in a coup on February 1, 2021. The military government sought legitimacy through strategic promotion of Buddhism. Buddhist nationalism rendered Muslims especially precarious, culminating in the Rohingya crisis and ethnic cleansing. In Karen State, sovereignty is splintered among ethnic armed organizations, local militias, Border Guard Forces, and the state military in a mosaic of territorial control.
myanmar.buddhistInstitutions: Theravada Buddhism is the dominant religion, deeply embedded in Burmese language (vocabulary draws heavily on Pali). The Sangha Maha Nayaka Committee is the government-appointed council of senior Buddhist monks that regulates religious terminology and practice. It issued statements against Christians appropriating Buddhist terms and attempted to ban Ma Ba Tha (Buddhist nationalist group). Christians were given a list of ~100 'Buddhist' words they were told not to use. Sitagu Sayadaw is one of Myanmar's most famous monks. Buddhism is intertwined with national belonging. In Karen State, charismatic monks like U Thuzana played key roles in legitimizing lay power structures, including building pagodas in Christian and Muslim compounds as Buddhist revivalism. Buddhism is the 'sonic tapestry of everyday life' -- sermons broadcast from monasteries are the first thing heard in the morning.
keyHistoricalMoments: Arrival of Adoniram Judson, American Baptist missionary, in Rangoon in 1813 -- brought first printing press and translated Bible into Burmese (completed 1834)
Ne Win's military rule from 1962 onward, expelling foreign missionaries
Press Scrutiny Board (est. 1962) required all printed materials submitted prior to publication
2015 NLD election victory / Aung San Suu Kyi's rise to power ending five decades of military rule
February 1, 2021 military coup
Ongoing political troubles in the Shan States driving migration into Thailand for over 100 years
DKBA (Democratic Karen Buddhist Army) formation and later integration as Border Guard Forces
U Thuzana's death in November 2018 after decades as charismatic patron of DKBA
distinctivePractices: Paya is a Burmanized Pali word used by Christians for 'God' and by Buddhists for the Buddha, creating anxiety about confusion
Nalehmu -- a political concept of 'mutual understanding,' personalized networks of reciprocity and trust that helped people survive military rule
Buddhist-Christian encounters involve complex translation dynamics: evangelists oscillate between 'domesticating' (using Buddhist concepts) and 'foreignizing' the gospel
The merit-power nexus: merit-making and wealth/social status are deeply intertwined; 'merit is power'
The Plong Karen concept of thout kyar ('keeping faithful') as core moral ideal -- faithfulness to self, others, community, and land
Money from 'unclean' sources (ma than shin te paik san) -- gambling, drugs, debt collection -- considered dangerous and morally polluting
U Thuzana encoded lottery numbers in Buddhist terminology and numerology during sermons
Spirits (nats) folded into Theravada practice alongside Buddhist observance
Morning prayer (awgatha) and daily metta recitations as universal devotional practice
Don (traditional Karen dance) performed at pagoda celebrations and cultural festivals
contemporaryIssues: State regulation of religious language: ~100 Pali-derived terms banned from non-Buddhist publications
Buddhist nationalism and anti-Muslim sentiment, including Rohingya ethnic cleansing
Former military/militia commanders using Buddhist patronage to legitimize wealth from gambling, drugs, and predatory lending
The moral complexity of 'dirty money' donations: lavish dana from strongmen simultaneously praised and critiqued
Youth warned against forgetting culture and religion amid modernization -- 'boyfriends, girlfriends, drinking, and drugs'
Christian evangelism in Buddhist neighborhoods using Buddhist vocabulary, creating tension over religious boundaries
Censorship of religious publications and control of religious terminology by state religious authorities

cambodia.politicalContext: Heirs of the monumental kingdom of Angkor (800s-1400s), Cambodia endured French colonialism from 1863, Cold War turmoil, US bombing, and the Khmer Rouge genocide (1975-1979) that killed approximately 1.7 million people. After long recuperation involving Vietnamese occupation and UN intervention, it survives as a small Buddhist kingdom with a constitutional monarch and a quasi-elected strongman prime minister who is a former Khmer Rouge commander. Democracy is highly compromised by authoritarian power structures. French colonial presence disturbed forests and mined mountains; post-genocide deforestation continued as government cut forests to deny Khmer Rouge refuge.
cambodia.buddhistInstitutions: The great Khmer kings of Angkor were both Hindu and Buddhist. The Angkor complex contains both Angkor Wat (Hindu) and the Bayon (Buddhist). Buddhism was nearly destroyed by the Khmer Rouge -- all religious practices were banned; monks who gave sermons on the First Precept were targeted. The modern Buddhist reformed movement was initiated by French colonial intent to 'civilize' indigenous religion, leading to the Buddhist Institute (est. 1930). In 1942, a monastic uprising against colonial rule was brutally suppressed. Neak Ta guardian spirit shrines are found even within Buddhist pagoda precincts. Maha Ghosananda (1913-2007) founded the Dhammayietra peace walk movement, Cambodia's most significant Buddhist peace initiative.
keyHistoricalMoments: Angkor's dominance over the Tonle Sap and lower Mekong basin (800s-1400s)
French colonial presence from 1863, mining mountains and granting concessions to foreign companies
Buddhist Institute established in 1930 under French colonial influence
1942 monastic uprising against colonial rule in Phnom Penh -- monks prosecuted for chanting Pali 'mantras of protection'
Khmer Rouge genocide (1975-1979): ~1.7 million killed, Buddhism and all religious practices banned
Elephants' exodus in 1975: herds fled northward to Laos and Thailand, said to sense the ukrittakamm (unbearable disaster)
First Dhammayietra peace walk in 1992: 430 km from Poipet to Phnom Penh starting at Site II refugee camp
1994 crossfire during third peace walk killed two pilgrims, a monk and a nun
Dhammayietra walks continued from 1992 to 2018, traversing landmined areas as acts of mindful walking
distinctivePractices: Dhammayietra (pilgrimage for truth / peace walk): Buddhist peace movement traversing conflict zones and landmined areas as mindful walking practice
Forest ordination: tying monastic robes to trees to make them sacred monks 'not to be killed or destroyed'
Robes tied to trees at Preah Vihear taken by villagers in 2006 and worn as amulets against landmines
Neak Ta guardian spirits: protectors of earth and water with shrines in Buddhist pagoda precincts
Prei (forest/jungle) as 'horrifying, unknowable, and undesirable' vs. Srok (village) as ordered space
Preay: the most vengeful ghost in Khmer animist tradition, with pronunciation close to Prei (forest)
Bangsokol: merit-making ceremony where deceased return to wander the earth for merit before Khmer New Year
Mali's mother's sacred vow (upakit) to the forest during Khmer Rouge starvation -- invoking dhamma and sacred beings for miraculous power
Maha Ghosananda related to Buddhist prophecy of Preah Bat Dhammik, the meritorious-charismatic leader who would heal Cambodia from the west
contemporaryIssues: Post-genocide trauma and the intersection of Buddhist healing with landmine-scarred landscapes
Deforestation as both environmental and spiritual crisis -- First Precept extended to include not 'killing' the forest
Environmental Buddhism: 'Our Dhammayietra office is anywhere under a tree'
Tension between forest conservation (sacred tree ordinations) and development pressure for agricultural clearing
Rebuilding Buddhist institutions and identity after near-total destruction under Khmer Rouge
Khmer Rouge legacy: the central faith that children should trust parents was replaced by ideology viewing 'old generation' as obstacles

vietnam.politicalContext: Vietnam's independence movement under Ho Chi Minh triumphed over France and the United States. The once domineering Confucian kingdom, colonized by France, rose as SE Asia's strongest Communist state. With 95+ million people, the Communist Party rules but market forces are dominant. The Communist government attempted to eliminate 'superstition' but religious practices persisted and revived since Doi Moi (Renovation, 1986). The war's massive death toll produced millions of angry spirits (con ma) shaping the spiritual landscape. Vietnam emerged under Chinese civilization's sway -- the Vietnamese homeland was a colony of China for nearly a thousand years. Economic reforms brought new forms of socioeconomic anxieties and precarity that drove increased religious activity.
vietnam.buddhistInstitutions: Buddhism in Vietnam is part of tam giao ('triple religion'): syncretic Taoism, Buddhism, and Confucianism, intertwined with indigenous animism. This is not institutionalized -- people don't identify as adherents but hold a generalized worldview. Distinction between ton giao (institutionalized religion) and tin nguong (popular beliefs/practices). Pagodas serve as sites for offerings and merit-making on lunar calendar dates. Vietnamese Buddhist practice is Mahayana (unlike Theravada mainland), including veneration of Bodhisattva Kuanyin and Daoist elements. The National Vietnam Buddhist Sangha was established in 1981. After the war ended in 1975, communist government shut down most Buddhist programs following 'atheist secularism.' Buddhist activities were limited to small-scale household chanting by older women until Doi Moi reforms.
keyHistoricalMoments: Nearly a thousand years of Chinese colonial rule (Han, Sui, Tang dynasties) shaping Confucian/Mahayana Buddhist culture
Dai Viet broke free from China in 939 CE
French colonization in the 19th century
The American War created massive numbers of angry ghosts (con ma) fundamentally shaping Vietnamese spiritual life
1920s-1970s: Vietnamese Buddhist Reform movement created journals, schools, youth groups, and social welfare institutions
Gia dinh Phat tu (Vietnam Buddhist Youth Family) had 70,000 members and 3,000 leaders by the 1960s
Thich Nhat Hanh founded the School of Youth for Social Services in 1965
1975: communist government shut down most Buddhist programs
1981: National Vietnam Buddhist Sangha established
1986: Doi Moi economic reforms allowed Buddhist activities to grow again
Mid-2000s: proliferation of Buddhist youth programs including weekend retreats, meditation, summer camps, and volunteering
distinctivePractices: Ban tho (ancestor altar): found in virtually every home regardless of religion; displays photos of deceased, incense, flowers, fruit offerings
Offerings to ancestors on 1st and 15th of each lunar month with favorite foods; higher caliber on Buddhist holidays
Ngay Xa Toi Vong Hon (Buddhist Feast of Wandering Souls / 'Hell Day'): 15th of 7th lunar month, families prepare special foods to pacify con ma
Nha tang (paper mansion): elaborate paper replicas burned as offerings to ancestors in the otherworld
Ancestor worship (tho cung to tien) not seen as 'religious' but as 'respect and intelligence'
Patrilineal kinship: daughters called 'flying ducks'; sons preferred to continue the patriline (ho)
Confucian ideal of ngu dai dong duong: five generations under one roof
The dinh (communal hall) as traditional village focal point housing the village guardian spirit
Tam bo nhat bai (three steps, one prostration): devotional practice from East Asian Mahayana and Tibetan Buddhism incorporated into retreats
Chanting liturgical texts in Sino-Vietnamese and transliterated Sanskrit -- even without comprehension -- believed to generate merit
Buddhist temples now offer wedding ceremonies as a relatively new phenomenon to attract young people
Young Vietnamese combine Buddhism with tarot cards, astrology, fortune telling, and self-improvement courses as 'projects of self-cultivation'
contemporaryIssues: Vietnamese Buddhism is 'mostly devotional' -- lay Buddhists go to temples on 1st and 15th lunar month to pray for well-being, academic success, romantic partnership
'Dharma illiteracy' (mu chu Phat phap): devotional focus has alienated young people, professionals, and intellectuals
Two competing forces: modernist Buddhism (philosophical, ethical, meditation-focused) vs. devotional Buddhism (supplication, chanting, merit-making rituals)
Class distinction in Buddhist approach: middle/upper-class educated youth attracted to modernist Buddhism; less educated retain devotional practices
Youth participation fluctuates: high during university, declining with jobs/families, returning during existential crises
Post-war urbanization creating tension between traditional ancestor obligations and modern career aspirations
Millions of angry spirits (con ma) from the war still shape the spiritual landscape
Buddhist youth programs proliferating since mid-2000s through temples like Giac Ngo and Temple of Harmony
Social media as key channel for Buddhist outreach -- temples use Facebook for announcements and content

laos.politicalContext: A traditionally Buddhist society formed from princely domains amalgamated into a French protectorate in the 1890s. Drawn into Vietnam's war via French Indochina, years of turmoil ended in 1975 with a Communist-led government. The Lao People's Democratic Republic remains a highly agrarian and largely Buddhist society. The lowland Lao majority populates narrow river valleys, but 90% of territory rises above 600 feet populated by non-Lao hill peoples. Globalization reaches deep from every side, especially China (40% of foreign investment). Competing national narratives -- communist, anticommunist, and others -- all lay claim to the nation's past. In northern Laos, multi-ethnic communities (Lue, Phounoy, Khmu, Akha) practice Theravada Buddhism alongside local spirit traditions.
laos.buddhistInstitutions: Buddhism coexists with diverse ethnic practices in multi-ethnic communities. The town of Boun Tay is historically a muang (small principality) from the Luang Prabang tradition. The acan (lay leader, a former monk) serves as the temple's ritual specialist who knows Buddhist texts and local traditions. Six young monks typically attend funeral ceremonies. The Lao state recognized Boun Tay as a district in the 1990s, bringing greater integration with national structures. Former royal capital Luang Prabang is renowned for its grace. Buddhist Lent (Khao Phansa) structures the three-month monsoon retreat period.
keyHistoricalMoments: Formation from princely domains (muang) in the Luang Prabang tradition
French protectorate from the 1890s
1975: Communist-led government established as Lao People's Democratic Republic
1990s: Boun Tay recognized as a district, bringing civil servants and national integration
Multiple waves of migration since the 19th century creating multiethnic communities in northern Laos
Cash crops (rubber, beans) now sold to Chinese entrepreneurs, shifting the subsistence rice economy
distinctivePractices: Phrasat ('palace' offering) ceremony: miniature house built at temple containing everything the deceased might need, the definitive ritual to establish the deceased as an ancestor
Song sakan ('sending away the corpse'): multi-day funeral ceremony with detailed ritual sequence including sompoi water purification, bamboo trellises (taleo) to deter spirits, and bone washing
Multiple soul concepts: khwan (vital essences, variable in number -- 9 or 32) and winnan (impermanent principle that transmigrates after death)
Soul-recalling ritual using a fish trap to retrieve souls of the living that may have contacted the deceased
Raw egg thrown to select reliquary location: 'if it's not the right place, the egg won't break'
Huean di ('house of joy'): the mourning house, signifying 'cessation of suffering in existence'
Severing of cotton thread connecting living spouse to coffin -- symbolizing broken ties like divorce
Sompoi water (Acacia concinna pods, blessed by monks) for purification throughout funeral ceremonies
Mak kua (thorny eggplant) leaves sprinkled at building corners to scare away spirits
Monks throw stones with sompoi water onto roofs to expel spirits
Khao Padapdin and Khao Salak: harvest ceremonies for the dead in the 9th and 10th lunar months
Boun Khaosalak: rice harvest offering ceremony concluding the agricultural cycle with ancestor offerings
Hair washing forbidden until after the funeral; post-funeral hair washing 'washes away all impurities' as rebirth symbol
Bone money (ngueune du) placed with cremated remains for the deceased's journey to paradise (muang savane)
contemporaryIssues: Connection between Buddhist calendar and agricultural rice cycle is weakening as employment diversifies
Ancestor offerings now made at convenient times (New Year) rather than traditional harvest-linked dates
Phrasat ceremony is indispensable but extremely costly -- some families wait many years to afford it
Funerary costs are significant (e.g., 150 kg of pork and a dozen ducks for one funeral)
Drug addiction among younger generation threatens continuity of ritual obligations
Cash crops replacing subsistence rice economy under Chinese commercial influence
Chinese companies building casinos, hotels, plantations, and hydroelectric dams across Laos

buddhistConcepts.description: The moral law of cause and effect. Every purposeful action registers on the cosmos and eventually returns to you. Karma is NOT fate -- it is a theory of moral causation placing responsibility on the individual. It determines circumstances of birth (human/animal, male/female, wealthy/poor) and death. Between birth and death, only dramatic events are typically explained by karma.
buddhistConcepts.gameRelevance: Core mechanic governing rebirth quality, life circumstances, and consequences of player choices. In Karen Plong Buddhism, moral subjectivity is 'based on the sum of all deeds performed, both in this and in previous lives.' Players accumulate positive and negative karma through every decision.

buddhistConcepts.description: Positive karmic stockpile earned through good deeds, especially institutionalized forms like gifts to the sangha. The amount of merit received is linked to the moral quality of the recipient -- monks are the best 'fields' for planting merit. Merit can be shared with the dead via yaat nam ritual, transferred to ancestors, and accumulated across lifetimes. Once dead, merit is the ONLY power that can reach a person.
buddhistConcepts.gameRelevance: Functions as a parallel currency/economy in the game. Earned through dana (giving to monks), sponsoring ceremonies, keeping precepts, ordination. Spent/transferred: shared with the dead, ensures good rebirth, protects against spirits. High merit = better rebirth, less susceptible to phi, more social respect. Creates a 'merit-power nexus' where merit begets social power and status.

buddhistConcepts.description: Negative karmic residue from immoral actions. Shan term wot refers specifically to negative karmic residue. A person who dies with no wot remaining had a spiritually clean life. Violent or sudden deaths create extra wot requiring additional merit-making. The phrase lai wot means 'to get one's due / receive retribution.'
buddhistConcepts.gameRelevance: Counterbalance to merit. Accumulated through bad choices, and must be addressed through extra merit-making. Affects quality of death (safe vs. dangerous) and rebirth. Dirty money (ma than shin te paik san) from gambling, drugs, or predatory lending carries heavy demerit.

buddhistConcepts.description: Injunctions against killing, thieving, lying, intoxication, and improper sexual behavior. Adults frequently remark that it is impossible to follow them daily until one is old. Moral behavior is, in some sense, postponed until later in life. A person who 'kept the precepts' is praised at death. Temple sleepers keep eight precepts (adding restrictions on eating, entertainment, and sleep comfort). In Cambodia, the First Precept has been extended to deforestation.
buddhistConcepts.gameRelevance: Moral framework that shifts across the life course. Young players struggle to keep precepts; older players gain status by keeping them strictly. Breaking precepts has karma consequences. The graduated precept system (5 for laypeople, 8 for devoted elders, 227 for monks) maps to player progression.

buddhistConcepts.description: The cycle of death and rebirth (samsara) driven by karma. After death, khwan become phi and must separate from the body to 'go to a good place.' People are concerned with both immediate fate (going to moeng phi / Buddhist heavens) and eventual rebirth in moeng khon (realm of humans). The quality of one's final thoughts determines rebirth quality. Vietnamese hell is not a place but 'a condition of homelessness and eternal hunger.'
buddhistConcepts.gameRelevance: Endgame mechanic determining the quality of the player's next incarnation. A 'good death' (old, meritorious, calm) leads to auspicious rebirth. A 'dangerous death' (young, violent, emotionally attached) creates spiritual crisis and worse rebirth prospects.

buddhistConcepts.description: Gift-giving that produces good karmic consequences, inseparable from Theravada devotion. The most important means through which people gain access to spiritual power. In Myanmar, dana can refer to wide range of good deeds: charity, food-giving, hospitality. The monastic donation economy creates a merit-power nexus linking merit, wealth, and social status. For women, everyday acts of merit-making provide spiritual opportunities and reinforce exemplary maternal qualities.
buddhistConcepts.gameRelevance: Primary action mechanic for earning merit. Daily food offerings to monks, sponsoring ceremonies, charitable acts all generate dana merit. Creates interesting tension: the most generous players gain the most respect but also attract the most requests from others (the asking-for-things dynamic). Generosity is socially enforced.

buddhistConcepts.description: Temporary ordination as a novice or monk is central to Theravada life. Young boys ordained as novices generate enormous merit for parents, especially mothers. It is the primary way a son 'repays' the debt to his mother -- women cannot ordain. Sponsoring ordination is a key marker of mature adulthood demonstrating competence, social maturity, and community responsibility. Three-day emergency ordinations can generate merit for the dying. In Laos, sons/grandsons may be temporarily ordained to transfer merit to deceased.
buddhistConcepts.gameRelevance: Major life event mechanic. For boys/young men: ordination is a coming-of-age event. For parents: sponsoring is a midlife milestone demonstrating maturity. For families: generates maximum merit. The lavishness of the ceremony reflects social standing. Creates gender tension: women cannot ordain but play central roles as sponsors.

buddhistConcepts.description: The 32 souls (variable: 9, 32, or other numbers by region) that collectively animate the body. Individually and collectively called khwan. When some are missing, the result is poor morale or sickness; when ALL are missing, the result is death. Khwan are skittish, easily startled, and prone to fleeing. Children's and women's souls are most tenuously attached. At death, khwan become phi. In rebirth, phi are transformed back into khwan. The body is a 'contested site' where opposing forces (phi vs. khwan) do battle.
buddhistConcepts.gameRelevance: Health/vitality system. Player's khwan can flee due to shock, grief, proximity to death, or spirit attack. Treatment requires both exorcism (pat phi) and soul-calling (haung khwan). Children and women have more vulnerable khwan. Emotional control protects khwan stability.

buddhistConcepts.description: Spirits/ghosts -- disembodied life force. The prototypical phi is a hungry, restless being caught in no-man's-land, unable to be reborn, hanging around edges of human settlements preying on the weak. In Vietnam, con ma are angry spirits created by war deaths; con quy are ghouls. In Cambodia, Preay is the most vengeful ghost. In Myanmar, nats are local spirits honored alongside Buddhism. Ancestral spirits (Vietnamese to tien) are benevolent if properly honored but can punish if neglected.
buddhistConcepts.gameRelevance: Antagonist/hazard system. Phi threaten player health and khwan stability. Different types require different responses: ancestral spirits need proper offerings, angry spirits need expert help, violent-death spirits require special rituals. The spirit world is an ever-present dimension of gameplay affecting choices about where to walk, what to eat, and how to grieve.

buddhistConcepts.description: A Vietnamese concept explaining why people feel drawn to particular temples, teachers, or places. Understood as consequences of past-life relationships causing predestined inclinations toward people, places, things, and preferences in this life. Used by young Buddhists to explain spiritual attraction and connection.
buddhistConcepts.gameRelevance: Can drive narrative branching -- the player's character feels inexplicable pull toward certain temples, mentors, or paths based on past-life connections, creating story hooks and meaningful choices about which communities to join.

buddhistConcepts.description: Ten principle virtues in Theravada Buddhism: charity, morality, renunciation, knowledge, effort, honesty, forbearance, loving-kindness, equanimity, and resolution. Among Plong Karen Buddhists, parami refers to virtue or aptitude toward enlightenment. The accumulation of parami across lifetimes determines spiritual progress.
buddhistConcepts.gameRelevance: Character development system -- ten virtues that can be cultivated through gameplay choices, each affecting different aspects of the player's karmic trajectory and community relationships.

buddhistConcepts.description: Buddhist ethical principle regarding moral ways of earning a living. Maps onto local Plong Karen concerns about 'unclean wealth' from gambling, drug sales, and debt collection. The concept of thout kyar encompasses faithfulness to self, others, community, and land, grounded in communal reciprocity.
buddhistConcepts.gameRelevance: Economic/career mechanic. Players must balance financial survival against moral integrity. Taking dirty money jobs pays 10x more but carries karmic consequences. The merit-power nexus means even ill-gotten wealth can be laundered through lavish dana, creating moral complexity.

buddhistConcepts.description: Anicca (impermanence), dukkha (suffering), and anatta (not-self). These three characteristics define all conditioned phenomena in Buddhist philosophy. Temple sleepers meditate on them using mak nap beads, repeating 'anicca, dukkha, anatta' in prescribed rounds. Understanding these marks is central to spiritual progress.
buddhistConcepts.gameRelevance: Philosophical framework underlying the entire game world. Impermanence means nothing is permanent -- wealth, relationships, health all change. Suffering motivates the search for merit and spiritual progress. Not-self challenges Western individualism and drives the communal nature of Buddhist practice.

buddhistConcepts.description: Liberation from the cycle of rebirth and suffering. The ultimate goal of Buddhist practice. The Burmese term hnjein means 'extinguished' -- used to describe nirvana. In practice, most laypeople focus on achieving better rebirth rather than nirvana itself, which is seen as the domain of monks and advanced practitioners.
buddhistConcepts.gameRelevance: Ultimate endgame goal, though most players will focus on the more immediate goal of a good rebirth. Achieving nirvana could be a rare, aspirational achievement for players who follow the monastic path through multiple life cycles.

buddhistConcepts.description: Merit can be shared with deceased relatives, hungry spirits, and all sentient beings. In Shan practice, yaat nam involves pouring water onto the ground while a monk chants, cosmically crediting merit to the deceased. In Laos, libations are poured as medium for transfer -- 'until this is done, the deceased cannot take possession.' Funeral rites are essentially communal merit-making for the deceased. The parable: 'When the monks eat the rice, his son eats the merit.'
buddhistConcepts.gameRelevance: Allows players to affect the spiritual welfare of deceased NPCs and ancestors. Creates obligations extending beyond death -- you must continue making merit for dead relatives. The phrasat ceremony in Laos and kotsaa in Shan culture are examples of mandatory post-death merit obligations.

buddhistConcepts.description: Excessive emotion is spiritually dangerous in Shan and broader Theravada worldview. Grief can 'pull' the dead back to harm the living. Emotional attachment makes khwan vulnerable to flight. Self-control is a lifelong project. At death, emotional readiness determines whether death is 'safe' or 'dangerous.' The preferred emotional expression is ilu man ('I feel sorry for them') rather than tsau lek ('I'm sad').
buddhistConcepts.gameRelevance: Emotional control mechanic. Players must manage grief, anger, and attachment. Excessive emotion at funerals endangers both the dead and the living. Older characters should have better emotional control. Failure to control emotions can cause khwan to flee, creating health consequences.

terminology.translation: No-self; the doctrine that no enduring soul or essence exists
terminology.context: One of the three marks of existence, recited during meditation

terminology.translation: Impermanence; all conditioned things are transient
