Attends monthly meetings with Thai provincial officials

encounterSeeds.title: The Sobriety Oath
encounterSeeds.situation: Your character has a drinking problem ruining their family life and reputation. A respected elder suggests 'drinking the water of the tsao moeng' -- a solemn oath before the village guardian spirit to never drink again.

choices.text: Take the oath -- risk supernatural punishment if broken (madness, illness, loss of money) but potential transformation and new spiritual abilities
choices.consequences: If kept: life transformation, possible spiritual calling as healer. If broken: severe supernatural punishment, social ruin.

choices.text: Refuse the oath and try to quit on your own -- lower risk but no supernatural backing
choices.consequences: Modest chance of recovery without spiritual support; family continues to suffer.

choices.text: Take the oath but eventually break it
choices.consequences: Severe consequences: spirit possession, social ruin, madness, illness from the tsao moeng's wrath.

encounterSeeds.stakes: Family stability, social reputation, potential spiritual calling, health
encounterSeeds.source: Scholar 1 (Eberhardt Ch2 -- Uncle Pon's transformation)

encounterSeeds.title: The Egg Won't Fall
encounterSeeds.situation: Your child is sick and the healer is performing a soul-calling ceremony. The egg keeps falling off the crossbow -- 15, 20, 25 attempts. The audience is getting restless. What does your child's khwan want?

choices.text: Offer money (modest cost)
choices.consequences: May work; moderate financial cost; shows reasonable concern.

choices.text: Offer flowers (low cost, sometimes works)
choices.consequences: Low investment; may not be enough for a demanding khwan.

choices.text: Offer your gold necklace or wedding ring (high cost, high chance of success)
choices.consequences: Significant financial sacrifice; high chance of healing; public display of generosity.

choices.text: Suggest the khwan wants a specific life change (becoming a monk, getting a spouse)
choices.consequences: Initiates a life path change for the child; reveals family dynamics publicly.

encounterSeeds.stakes: Child's health, family wealth, public display of desperation/generosity
encounterSeeds.source: Scholar 1 (Eberhardt Ch2 -- Nang Ing and Lom's healing sessions)

encounterSeeds.title: Someone Asks for Your Food
encounterSeeds.situation: You're eating a meal and a neighbor/stranger makes eye contact and approaches. The cultural expectation is clear: you must share.

choices.text: Share generously
choices.consequences: Merit gain, reputation boost, resource loss.

choices.text: Claim 'am mii' -- 'I don't have any' (only works if food isn't visible)
choices.consequences: Works if food is hidden; lying damages merit if caught.

choices.text: Hide your food before they get close
choices.consequences: Socially risky if caught; seen as devious.

choices.text: Refuse outright
choices.consequences: Severe reputation damage; seen as claiming high status without generosity to back it up.

encounterSeeds.stakes: Resources, social reputation, hierarchical relationships, the leveling mechanism of generosity
encounterSeeds.source: Scholar 1 (Eberhardt Ch4 -- asking-for-things dynamic)

encounterSeeds.title: A Violent Death in Your Community
encounterSeeds.situation: A young mother and child are killed suddenly in a freak accident. The village is in turmoil. The kamnan asks for volunteers to help with immediate cremation.

choices.text: Volunteer for the cremation crew (requires liquor for bravery; risk to your own khwan)
choices.consequences: Builds reputation for courage and community service; risk to spiritual health.

choices.text: Stay home and lock your windows
choices.consequences: Safe but seen as cowardly; your khwan is protected.

choices.text: Go to the temple to make merit for the dead
choices.consequences: Safe, virtuous, but less brave than volunteering.

choices.text: Offer to sponsor extra merit-making ceremonies
choices.consequences: Expensive but generates enormous social credit and spiritual benefit for the deceased.

encounterSeeds.stakes: Community standing, spiritual safety, emotional well-being, khwan stability
encounterSeeds.source: Scholar 1 (Eberhardt Ch3 -- Nang Yen and Tsip's dangerous death)

encounterSeeds.title: Your Child Stole from the Ancestor Altar
encounterSeeds.situation: Your children have been sneaking food from the ancestor altar for months. Now both are experiencing nightmares, convulsions, and mysterious illness. Your grandmother discovers the theft.

choices.text: Enforce traditional punishment: fasting and enforced silence for a month
choices.consequences: Harsh but culturally correct; symptoms resolve; ancestor spirits appeased.

choices.text: Take children to the provincial hospital
choices.consequences: Modern approach; doctors find nothing; symptoms continue.

choices.text: Consult a spirit medium
choices.consequences: Costs money; may reveal additional information about what ancestors want.

choices.text: Make extra-elaborate offerings to appease the ancestors
choices.consequences: Expensive but addresses root cause; restores ancestor relations.

encounterSeeds.stakes: Children's health, ancestor relations, family harmony, generational conflict about tradition vs. modernity
encounterSeeds.source: Scholar 1 (Gustafsson -- Phan Van Si and My Hanh)

encounterSeeds.title: Your Daughter in the City Neglects Ancestor Duties
encounterSeeds.situation: Your eldest daughter has moved to the city for work. She earns good money and supports the family, but didn't come home for Tet/New Year. You start having night terrors. The old ancestral home's altar has been neglected by new tenants.

choices.text: Call your daughter home immediately to perform rites (risk to her career)
choices.consequences: Ancestors likely appeased; daughter's career may suffer.

choices.text: Perform the rites yourself as a proxy
choices.consequences: May not satisfy ancestors since she has 'senior' status.

choices.text: Commission a nha tang (paper mansion) as offering
choices.consequences: Expensive but dramatic gesture; may fully appease ancestors.

choices.text: Accept the situation and endure the symptoms
choices.consequences: Risky -- ancestors could escalate their displeasure.

encounterSeeds.stakes: Family health, ancestor satisfaction, daughter's career, household finances
encounterSeeds.source: Scholar 1 (Gustafsson -- Nguyen Thi Thuy's career vs. ancestor duty)

encounterSeeds.title: Sponsoring an Ordination
encounterSeeds.situation: Your son is old enough to be ordained as a novice. Sponsoring the ceremony is a major life milestone announcing your maturity and standing in the community.

choices.text: Sponsor a lavish, village-wide ordination festival
choices.consequences: Enormous expense but maximum merit for parents, maximum social prestige.

choices.text: Sponsor a modest ceremony
choices.consequences: Respectable but not impressive; some may question your ambition.

choices.text: Delay the ordination
choices.consequences: People question your maturity and readiness for community responsibility.

choices.text: Co-sponsor with another family to share costs and build alliances
choices.consequences: Saves money, builds alliances, but shared glory.

encounterSeeds.stakes: Parental merit (especially mother's), community reputation, demonstration of mature adulthood, financial resources
encounterSeeds.source: Scholar 1 (Eberhardt Ch6 -- ordination festivals)

encounterSeeds.title: The Dying Elder
encounterSeeds.situation: An elderly relative is dying. The monks say they accumulated great merit and have no wot. The family gathers.

choices.text: Control your grief publicly (culturally correct; strong emotions could pull the dying person's phi back)
choices.consequences: Protects the deceased's rebirth and your own khwan stability.

choices.text: Weep openly (natural but dangerous; emotional attachment creates spiritual tether)
choices.consequences: Your grief may hinder the deceased's passage; endangers your own khwan.

choices.text: Organize immediate merit-making at the temple
choices.consequences: Proactive, virtuous; demonstrates maturity.

choices.text: Begin arranging funeral logistics
choices.consequences: Practical, expected of mature adults; shows competence.

encounterSeeds.stakes: The deceased's rebirth, your own khwan stability, community evaluation of your emotional maturity
encounterSeeds.source: Scholar 1 (Eberhardt Ch3 -- Grandfather Wang's good death)

encounterSeeds.title: The Healer's Calling
encounterSeeds.situation: After a spiritual crisis (perhaps following drinking the tsao moeng's water), you begin having dreams in which powerful healing verses (katha) come to you unbidden. People in the village say the tsao moeng has chosen you.

choices.text: Accept the calling: begin attending temple on wan sin, set up as a saraa (healer)
choices.consequences: Life path change; new income stream; spiritual responsibility and community service.

choices.text: Resist the calling
choices.consequences: Dangerous: the tsao moeng chose you for a reason; ignoring may bring punishment.

choices.text: Seek guidance from an established healer (safe middle path; mentorship)
choices.consequences: Learns from experience; builds relationship with mentor; gradual transition.

encounterSeeds.stakes: Life direction, spiritual power, community role, relationship with tsao moeng
encounterSeeds.source: Scholar 1 (Eberhardt Ch2 -- Uncle Pon's calling)

encounterSeeds.title: Gambling at a Funeral
encounterSeeds.situation: You're at a neighbor's funeral vigil. Gambling games beneath the house are lively. You have limited cash.

choices.text: Gamble modestly (social bonding, keeps the vigil going)
choices.consequences: Risk of loss; helps community function; 'if we didn't have gambling, not enough people would come.'

choices.text: Gamble big (risk of financial ruin but might win)
choices.consequences: Demonstrates boldness; high risk; potential winnings or devastating loss.

choices.text: Don't gamble, stay upstairs with monks and elders
choices.consequences: Pious but misses the social scene; gains merit.

choices.text: Set up a food stall selling sweets and roasted eggs to the gamblers
choices.consequences: Entrepreneurial option; earn money serving others; respectable side hustle.

encounterSeeds.stakes: Money, social integration, entrepreneurial opportunity, spiritual merit
encounterSeeds.source: Scholar 1 (Eberhardt Ch3 -- funeral gambling traditions)

encounterSeeds.title: The Hunter's Omens
encounterSeeds.situation: You are an aging hunter/fisher in a Shan village. Lately you've experienced strange omens: missed shots at close range, empty fishing nets, a fish that trapped your hand. Village elders say these are signs you should give up hunting and become a temple sleeper.

choices.text: Heed the omens and begin the transition to temple sleeping -- give up hunting, take the eight precepts
choices.consequences: Karmic progression; community respect; spiritual development; loss of hunter identity and income.

choices.text: Acknowledge the omens but delay -- 'there are still things I need to teach my apprentice'
choices.consequences: Reasonable middle path; risks further omens; community may be patient.

choices.text: Dismiss the omens as coincidence and continue hunting
choices.consequences: Risks spiritual consequences; killing generates demerit; community disapproval.

encounterSeeds.stakes: Karmic progression vs. worldly attachment; community respect vs. personal identity; spiritual development timing
encounterSeeds.source: Scholar 2 (Eberhardt Ch7 -- Uncle Toen the Great Hunter)

encounterSeeds.title: The Novice Ordination Decision
encounterSeeds.situation: You are a 10-year-old Shan boy. Your village is holding a novice ordination festival. Your parents expect you to ordain. Your friend is excited, but you are worried about sleeping away from home and not eating after noon.

choices.text: Embrace the ordination enthusiastically
choices.consequences: Maximum merit for parents; strong Buddhist identity formation; community pride.

choices.text: Go through with it reluctantly, focusing on the festival aspects
choices.consequences: Still generates merit; ambivalence is normal; the act of anticipation itself shapes identity.

choices.text: Try to delay or avoid it
choices.consequences: Creates family tension; seen as immature; delays merit generation for parents.

encounterSeeds.stakes: Family honor, community expectations, personal fear vs. cultural duty; beginning of Buddhist identity formation
encounterSeeds.source: Scholar 2 (Eberhardt Ch8 -- Tsit and Weng anticipating ordination)

encounterSeeds.title: The Temple Sleeper's Scandal
encounterSeeds.situation: You are an elderly temple sleeper known for your blessings and healing powers. One evening, you lose your temper at a grandchild and are seen by neighbors. A village gossip spreads the word.

choices.text: Acknowledge the lapse publicly and recommit to the precepts with visible austerity
choices.consequences: Maintains respect through humility; demonstrates true spiritual maturity.

choices.text: Ignore the gossip and hope it passes
choices.consequences: May work short-term but temple sleepers are held to higher standards; lingering damage.

choices.text: Use your spiritual authority to redirect the narrative
choices.consequences: May work politically but risks seeming manipulative; tests genuine vs. performative spirituality.

encounterSeeds.stakes: Temple sleepers held to higher moral standards -- loss of respect means loss of perceived spiritual power and community role
encounterSeeds.source: Scholar 2 (Eberhardt Ch7 -- temple sleeper moral standards)

encounterSeeds.title: The Paying Respect Ceremony
encounterSeeds.situation: It is the biannual kaentau ceremony. As a young person, you must decide which temple sleepers to visit. There are fifty in your village and you cannot visit them all.

choices.text: Visit only close relatives and neighbors (practical but potentially disrespectful to others)
choices.consequences: Efficient but narrow; some elders may feel slighted.

choices.text: Try to visit as many as possible, rushing through each encounter
choices.consequences: Shows effort but shallow engagement; blessings may be less powerful.

choices.text: Focus on a few key elders known for powerful blessings, bringing extra-generous gifts
choices.consequences: Strategic; maximizes spiritual benefit; builds strong relationships with powerful elders.

encounterSeeds.stakes: Social relationships, community standing, accumulated merit through respectful gestures
encounterSeeds.source: Scholar 2 (Eberhardt Ch7 -- kaentau Paying Respect ceremony)

encounterSeeds.title: The Drowning Man Tract
encounterSeeds.situation: You are a Buddhist passenger on a boat traveling down the Irrawaddy River. A violent storm breaks out. A Christian stranger begins handing out pamphlets showing a drowning person.

choices.text: Take the pamphlet as a terrible omen, throw it away, and demand the person stop
choices.consequences: Follows superstitious instinct; creates conflict with the evangelist; other passengers may agree.

choices.text: Politely accept it but feel disturbed -- is this a warning about the storm?
choices.consequences: Anxiety increases; the pamphlet's imagery haunts you.

choices.text: Read it curiously -- what does this foreign religion offer?
choices.consequences: Opens a window to interfaith encounter; may challenge or strengthen your Buddhist identity.

encounterSeeds.stakes: Superstition vs. curiosity; interfaith encounter; the meaning of omens during danger
encounterSeeds.source: Scholar 2 (Edwards -- The Drowning Man tract on the Irrawaddy)

encounterSeeds.title: The Evangelist at Your Door
encounterSeeds.situation: A young Christian woman knocks on your door in peri-urban Yangon. She begins speaking about suffering (dukkha), how merit (kutho) is never enough, and how grace (che zu daw) from Jesus can deliver you to 'everlasting heaven.' She uses Buddhist vocabulary but her message is clearly Christian.

choices.text: 'All religions are the same (tu tu)' -- politely agree and disengage
choices.consequences: Deflects the encounter gracefully; maintains Buddhist equanimity; frustrates evangelist.

choices.text: Engage seriously with the theological differences between merit and grace
choices.consequences: Deep interfaith dialogue; may strengthen or challenge your understanding.

choices.text: Become offended that she is appropriating Buddhist terms and tell her to stop
choices.consequences: Defends Buddhist linguistic boundaries; creates conflict; reflects state policy tensions.

choices.text: Invite her to visit the monastery with you instead
choices.consequences: Generous, hospitable response; reverses the dynamic; demonstrates Buddhist confidence.

encounterSeeds.stakes: Buddhist identity, openness to other traditions, the politics of religious language in Myanmar
encounterSeeds.source: Scholar 2 (Edwards -- Daw Than Than's encounter with evangelists)

encounterSeeds.title: The Sacred Shrine vs. Urban Development
encounterSeeds.situation: The government announces plans to demolish a beloved saint's shrine in your neighborhood to build a new development. The shrine has stood for over 150 years and is a pilgrimage site. Workers are already arriving with bulldozers.

choices.text: Join the devotees gathering to protect the shrine, telling miracle stories about what happened to others who tried to destroy it
choices.consequences: Defends sacred heritage; risks confrontation with authorities; strengthens community bonds.

choices.text: Accept the government's decision but negotiate relocation of sacred relics
choices.consequences: Pragmatic compromise; may partially preserve sacred space; some community disappointment.

choices.text: See the development as progress and modernity
choices.consequences: Aligns with modernization; alienates traditionalist community members.

encounterSeeds.stakes: Sacred heritage vs. modernization; community identity; who controls sacred space
encounterSeeds.source: Scholar 2 (Sevea -- Sharifa Siti Maryam shrine demolition in Singapore)

encounterSeeds.title: The Royal Textual Assembly
encounterSeeds.situation: You are a minor lord in 15th-century Chiang Mai. The king has called for a grand Buddhist editorial assembly at his new monastery. Attending means pledging loyalty and resources. But the rival king of Ayutthaya has also invited monks from your region.

choices.text: Attend Tilokaraja's assembly at Chiang Mai -- local loyalty
choices.consequences: Strengthens local alliance; gains merit through textual patronage; political safety.

choices.text: Accept Trailokaraja of Ayutthaya's invitation -- potentially more powerful patron
choices.consequences: Risks local betrayal; gains access to larger political network; uncertain loyalties.

choices.text: Send representatives to both while remaining uncommitted
choices.consequences: Hedges bets; may be seen as lacking conviction; keeps options open.

encounterSeeds.stakes: Political allegiance through religious patronage; which Buddhist textual lineage to support; spiritual merit and political power intersect
encounterSeeds.source: Scholar 2 (Blackburn -- Tilokaraja's Buddhist editorial assembly)

encounterSeeds.title: The Aging Decision
encounterSeeds.situation: You are in your mid-forties. Your physical labor is slowing. People have started calling you 'old' and asking when you'll start sleeping at the temple. But your house isn't finished, your last child hasn't finished school, and you need cash.

choices.text: Begin the transition now -- start keeping precepts on wan sin, gradually withdraw from farm labor
choices.consequences: Spiritual progression; community approval; financial strain; role model for younger generation.

choices.text: Delay until practical matters are resolved -- 'after the house, after school'
choices.consequences: Reasonable but risks indefinite postponement; community patience wears thin.

choices.text: Resist the label entirely -- you feel too young for 'old age'
choices.consequences: Personal autonomy but community friction; age identity is socially assigned, not individually chosen.

encounterSeeds.stakes: Material obligations vs. spiritual progression; community's developmental timeline vs. personal readiness; karmic investment in this life vs. next
encounterSeeds.source: Scholar 2 (Eberhardt Ch7 -- aging and temple sleeping transitions)

encounterSeeds.title: The Buddhist Word Police
encounterSeeds.situation: You are a Christian pastor in Myanmar. The Sangha Maha Nayaka Committee has banned non-Buddhists from using ~100 Pali-derived words in publications, including metta, dukkha, and paya (which you use for God).

choices.text: Comply fully and develop entirely new Christian vocabulary, risking incomprehensibility
choices.consequences: Legal safety; communication difficulty; congregants confused by unfamiliar terms.

choices.text: Continue using the terms discreetly, risking legal consequences
choices.consequences: Maintains communication clarity; legal danger; defiance of state religious authority.

choices.text: Adopt a middle path -- 'domesticate' some terms while 'foreignizing' others
choices.consequences: Balanced approach; some terms gain new meanings; ongoing negotiation with both communities.

choices.text: Challenge the ruling publicly as censorship
choices.consequences: Brave but extremely risky in Myanmar's political context; could galvanize support or bring persecution.

encounterSeeds.stakes: Minority religious survival, translation as power, the boundary between religions in a Buddhist-majority state
encounterSeeds.source: Scholar 2 (Edwards -- state regulation of religious language in Myanmar)

encounterSeeds.title: The Funeral Dilemma
encounterSeeds.situation: A family member has died. You must organize the funeral, but keeping the body at home for the most auspicious timing costs significantly more (food for many visitors over multiple days).

choices.text: Wait for the most auspicious day (5 days) -- maximum merit and proper passage but much higher costs
choices.consequences: Maximum spiritual benefit; community participation; significant financial burden; proper calendar alignment.

choices.text: Cremate quickly (1-2 days) -- saves money but may not be the right calendar day
choices.consequences: Financial relief but potential spiritual complications; acan may disapprove.

choices.text: Ask the community to collect contributions from every family in the district
choices.consequences: Communal support; distributes costs; builds reciprocal obligations; shows humility.

encounterSeeds.stakes: Family finances, the deceased's journey to paradise, community standing, protection of the living's khwan from contact with death
encounterSeeds.source: Scholar 3 (Boute -- funeral at Boun Tay)

encounterSeeds.title: The Phrasat Obligation
encounterSeeds.situation: Years have passed since your parent's death. You cannot afford the phrasat (palace offering) -- the definitive ritual to establish the deceased as ancestor. Your parent appears in dreams. Your sibling's spouse wants to remarry, requiring the phrasat.

choices.text: Take on debt to perform the ceremony now
choices.consequences: Fulfills obligation; frees sibling to remarry; financial hardship; deceased becomes settled ancestor.

choices.text: Wait and continue making smaller offerings during Buddhist Lent holidays
choices.consequences: Manageable financially but deceased remains unsettled; sibling cannot remarry; dreams continue.

choices.text: Transfer the responsibility to your own children
choices.consequences: Defers burden to next generation; children may not have resources; tradition says 'you cannot skip phrasat.'

encounterSeeds.stakes: The deceased's final status in the afterlife, family obligations, financial stability, a sibling's future marriage prospects
encounterSeeds.source: Scholar 3 (Boute -- phrasat obligation and remarriage case)

encounterSeeds.title: The Strongman's Donation
encounterSeeds.situation: A wealthy local figure -- known to control gambling and drug operations -- offers to sponsor a major pagoda celebration in your village and fund the ordination of several local boys as novice monks.

choices.text: Accept the sponsorship gratefully -- his dana generates merit for everyone and supports the sasana
choices.consequences: Community religious infrastructure funded; boys gain ordination merit; you become part of his patronage network.

choices.text: Politely decline -- 'unclean money' from gambling and drugs will bring bad karma
choices.consequences: Moral integrity preserved; community may lose out on infrastructure; risk of offending a powerful person.

choices.text: Accept but distance yourself personally from the sponsor
choices.consequences: Pragmatic compromise; attend ceremony but avoid patronage obligations; walking a fine line.

encounterSeeds.stakes: Community religious infrastructure, moral standing (thout kyar), risk of patronage entanglement, merit for ordaining boys' families
encounterSeeds.source: Scholar 3 (Chambers -- Saw Eh Klein the Buddhist strongman)

encounterSeeds.title: The Lottery Sermon
encounterSeeds.situation: A powerful charismatic monk is giving a sermon. At the end, he encodes lottery numbers in Buddhist recitation and numerology. Your family is deeply in debt.

choices.text: Try to decipher the numbers and gamble -- 'only those with appropriate karma can find the winning number'
choices.consequences: Possible windfall or deeper debt; tests your faith in karmic destiny; gambling contradicts precepts.

choices.text: Focus only on the dhamma teachings and ignore the lottery aspect
choices.consequences: Spiritually pure response; misses potential financial relief; gains genuine merit from hearing dhamma.

choices.text: Attend for the merit of devotion but avoid gambling entirely
choices.consequences: Shows devotion without compromising Right Livelihood; family debt persists.

encounterSeeds.stakes: Family debt, spiritual merit, the line between faith and gambling addiction, relationship with karma
encounterSeeds.source: Scholar 3 (Chambers -- U Thuzana's lottery sermons)

encounterSeeds.title: Transgender Ordination
encounterSeeds.situation: You identify as katoey (transgender). You feel a strong calling to monastic life and spiritual practice. The village temple will not accept you.

choices.text: Suppress your gender identity and ordain as a male bhikkhu (227 rules)
choices.consequences: Full ordination status; denial of self; potential internal suffering despite external legitimacy.

choices.text: Take eight precepts as a mae chii (nun) -- maintaining femininity but accepting subordinate status
choices.consequences: Preserves gender expression; accepts institutional marginalization; ambiguous status.

choices.text: Seek out a 'third-way' monastery like Dantham that accepts transgender practitioners
choices.consequences: Authentic spiritual path; smaller community; potential for genuine acceptance and growth.

choices.text: Remain a dedicated lay practitioner and practice independently
choices.consequences: No institutional support; personal freedom; misses the merit-generating power of ordination for family.

encounterSeeds.stakes: Spiritual path, gender identity, community acceptance, family merit (ordination sends parents to heaven), institutional support
encounterSeeds.source: Scholar 3 (Kidpromma -- transgender monasticism and Khun Mae Tritrinn)

encounterSeeds.title: The Nunnery Under Siege
encounterSeeds.situation: You've established a small meditation center as a transgender monastic teacher. The local community suspects you bring bad luck (khued). Villagers chase you away during morning alms. An influential royal temple demands you stop wearing saffron robes.

choices.text: Comply and switch to white mae chii robes -- maintain peace but lose symbolic equality
choices.consequences: Community friction decreases; institutional legitimacy weakened; followers may feel betrayed.

choices.text: Keep wearing saffron and face continued conflict with the establishment
choices.consequences: Principled defiance; ongoing hostility; models courage for followers; risks center's survival.

choices.text: Switch to brown robes (reformed forest monk lineage) as a compromise
choices.consequences: Creative middle path; connects to respected forest tradition; neither full compliance nor defiance.

choices.text: Move the center to a more remote location away from the village
choices.consequences: Reduces conflict; harder for followers to reach; echoes the forest monk tradition of withdrawal.

encounterSeeds.stakes: Institutional legitimacy, community relations, self-expression, center's survival, followers who depend on it
encounterSeeds.source: Scholar 3 (Kidpromma -- Khun Mae Tritrinn's rejection and perseverance)

encounterSeeds.title: Forest vs. Development
encounterSeeds.situation: A forested area near your village is scheduled for agricultural clearing. Monks have previously ordained the trees (tied monastic robes around them). Local developers want the land.

choices.text: Support the tree ordination and oppose clearing -- 'abstinence from killing' extends to forests
choices.consequences: Defends environmental dhamma; risks conflict with developers; preserves sacred space.

choices.text: Allow the clearing but ensure replanting elsewhere
choices.consequences: Pragmatic compromise; community gets agricultural land; sacred connection to specific place is lost.

choices.text: Organize a Dhammayietra-style peace walk through the forest to raise awareness
choices.consequences: Public action; builds community support; mindful walking as spiritual practice; may delay or prevent clearing.

choices.text: Take the robes from the trees and use them as protective amulets (as villagers did at Preah Vihear)
choices.consequences: Repurposes sacred objects; personal protection gained; forest loses its ordained status.

encounterSeeds.stakes: Environmental preservation, livelihood needs, Buddhist ethics (First Precept extended to nature), sacred space
encounterSeeds.source: Scholar 3 (Kittisenee -- forest ordination and environmental Buddhism)

encounterSeeds.title: The Peace Walk Through Landmines
encounterSeeds.situation: You've been invited to join a Dhammayietra peace walk. The route passes through former conflict zones with possible unexploded ordnance. The walk is an act of mindful walking and Buddhist healing.

choices.text: Join the walk -- practice mindfulness literally, as monks originally did on landmined paths
choices.consequences: Profound spiritual practice; genuine danger; contribution to post-conflict healing; maximum merit.

choices.text: Join but insist on staying within roped-off safe areas
choices.consequences: Participates with reduced risk; less transformative experience; still contributes to the movement.

choices.text: Support the walk financially and spiritually but don't physically participate
choices.consequences: Safe contribution; generates dana merit; misses the embodied practice of mindful walking.

choices.text: Volunteer to help with tree ordinations at the walk's conclusion
choices.consequences: Combines peace-making with environmental protection; practical contribution.

encounterSeeds.stakes: Personal safety, spiritual practice, contribution to post-conflict healing, merit-making (walks coincide with bangsokol season)
encounterSeeds.source: Scholar 3 (Kittisenee -- Dhammayietra peace walks through landmined areas)

encounterSeeds.title: Youth Buddhist Retreat
encounterSeeds.situation: You're a university student in Ho Chi Minh City. A temple offers a weekend retreat combining Dharma lectures, meditation, and a talk show with a successful celebrity businessperson. Your exams are next week.

choices.text: Attend the retreat -- spiritual cultivation is 'total life practice' that will help in all areas
choices.consequences: Spiritual growth; social networking (career opportunities at temple events); exam preparation suffers.

choices.text: Skip the retreat and study -- practical obligations come first
choices.consequences: Academic focus; misses spiritual community; can attend next month.

choices.text: Attend only the meditation portion and skip the celebrity talk show
choices.consequences: Balanced approach; genuine spiritual practice without spectacle; partial participation.

choices.text: Go to the retreat and combine it with consulting a tarot reader about your exam results
choices.consequences: Syncretic approach common among Vietnamese youth; mixes Buddhist and popular spiritual practices.

encounterSeeds.stakes: Academic performance, spiritual growth, social networking, duyen (karmic connection) with the temple community
encounterSeeds.source: Scholar 3 (Nguyen -- youth Buddhist programs in HCMC)

encounterSeeds.title: Devotional vs. Modernist Buddhism
encounterSeeds.situation: Your grandmother wants you to come to the temple on the 1st of the lunar month to make offerings and pray to the Bodhisattva of Compassion. Your modernist Buddhist friend says this is 'superstitious' and that real Buddhism is about meditation and philosophy.

choices.text: Go with your grandmother -- devotional practice has its own merit and strengthens family bonds
choices.consequences: Family harmony; merit-making; respects tradition; modernist friend may judge you.

choices.text: Go to the modernist temple's Vipassana session instead
choices.consequences: Philosophical depth; meditation practice; grandmother feels abandoned.

choices.text: Go with grandmother but silently reinterpret devotional elements through a modernist lens
choices.consequences: Both family bond and personal philosophy maintained; internal reconciliation of two approaches.

choices.text: Skip both and volunteer at the temple's charity organization instead
choices.consequences: Practical merit-making; serves the community; avoids the devotional/modernist debate.

encounterSeeds.stakes: Family harmony, generational tensions, personal spiritual development, relationship between merit-making and philosophical understanding
encounterSeeds.source: Scholar 3 (Nguyen -- modernism vs. devotionalism in Vietnamese Buddhism)

encounterSeeds.title: Dirty Money and Right Livelihood
encounterSeeds.situation: In your village, a wealthy boss (thae hti) offers you a job managing his gambling and money-lending operation. The pay would be 10x what you earn farming rice. Your family is struggling.

choices.text: Accept the job -- you can use the money for dana and merit-making to offset the karma
choices.consequences: Financial relief; moral compromise; attempting to launder dirty money through merit; dangerous precedent.

choices.text: Refuse -- 'dirty money will bring suffering to you and your family'
choices.consequences: Moral integrity (thout kyar) preserved; family continues to struggle; community respect earned.

choices.text: Accept temporarily to pay off debts, then return to farming
choices.consequences: Pragmatic but risky; debt spirals and addiction may trap you; 'temporary' rarely stays temporary.

choices.text: Ask the village monastery's monks for advice
choices.consequences: Wise counsel; community support; monks may offer alternative solutions; shows humility.

encounterSeeds.stakes: Family survival, moral integrity (thout kyar), right livelihood (samma-ajiva), community reputation, risk of debt spirals
encounterSeeds.source: Scholar 3 (Chambers -- unclean money and the strongman economy in Karen State)

encounterSeeds.title: The Deceased's Spirit Won't Rest
encounterSeeds.situation: After your mother's funeral, strange things happen in the house. Objects move, family members feel unwell, you have vivid dreams of your mother asking for food and clothing.

choices.text: Contact the acan (lay leader) to perform a merit-transfer ceremony at the temple
choices.consequences: Formal ritual approach; merit transferred to deceased; acan's expertise guides the process.

choices.text: Place daily food offerings at the household altar and address the deceased directly
choices.consequences: Personal, immediate response; may partially satisfy the spirit; doesn't resolve the underlying issue.

choices.text: Have monks throw sompoi water with river stones on the roof and place bamboo trellises at the corners
choices.consequences: Protective ritual cleansing; deters malevolent spirits; creates safe spiritual boundary.

choices.text: Begin saving for the phrasat ceremony to definitively settle the deceased
choices.consequences: Addresses root cause; long-term solution; financial commitment; 'where he is, he will have everything.'

encounterSeeds.stakes: Family health and well-being, the deceased's spiritual journey, ritual obligations, balance between mourning and moving on
encounterSeeds.source: Scholar 3 (Boute -- post-funeral spirit disturbances and the phrasat obligation)

crossCuttingThemes.theme: The Life Course as Game Structure
crossCuttingThemes.description: The Shan/Theravada life course provides a natural game progression framework. Childhood: vulnerable khwan, learning hierarchical relationships (pi-naung), acquiring cultural knowledge. Adolescence: temporary ordination as novice, education, first relationships. Young adulthood: marriage, household formation, building economic base. Midlife: sponsoring ordination festivals (key milestone), community leadership, increased generosity. Old age: temple sleeping, intensive merit accumulation, emotional control, preparation for death. The order of the sequence matters more than chronological age -- people move through phases at different rates.
crossCuttingThemes.gameDesignImplication: Structure the game around life stages rather than calendar years. Each stage unlocks new encounter types, social roles, and spiritual opportunities. The player's progression through stages is driven by family transitions (marriage, children's ordination) rather than age alone.

crossCuttingThemes.description: Merit (kuso/kutho/bun/boung) functions as a parallel spiritual currency earned through dana, precept-keeping, ordination sponsorship, and good deeds. It can be shared with the dead via water-pouring rituals and libations. High merit yields better rebirth, social respect, spiritual powers, and protection from spirits. The 'merit-power nexus' means merit begets social power and status, which begets more merit. 'Unclean money' from immoral sources cannot be properly laundered through merit-making -- or can it?
crossCuttingThemes.gameDesignImplication: Implement merit as a visible spiritual resource alongside material wealth. Merit and money interact in complex ways: generous spending generates merit, but dirty money corrupts the exchange. Create tension between accumulating wealth and giving it away. The merit economy should be the primary driver of long-term outcomes (rebirth quality).

crossCuttingThemes.description: The Shan view excessive emotion as spiritually dangerous. Grief can 'pull' the dead back to harm the living. Emotional attachment makes khwan vulnerable to flight. Self-control (the 'domestication of the self') is a lifelong project that intensifies in old age. At death, emotional readiness determines whether death is 'safe' or 'dangerous.' The preferred expression is compassion (ilu man / 'I pity them') rather than personal grief (tsau lek / 'I'm sad').
crossCuttingThemes.gameDesignImplication: Create an emotional control mechanic that players must manage. Strong emotions (grief, anger, attachment) have real gameplay consequences: khwan instability, spiritual vulnerability, and danger to loved ones. Older characters should have better emotional regulation. This creates a counterintuitive dynamic where caring less (emotionally) is spiritually better.

crossCuttingThemes.description: Women cannot ordain as bhikkhus in Theravada traditions, making their primary path to merit the sponsoring of sons' ordinations. Women play central roles in funeral organization, food preparation, daily almsgiving, and community labor -- they are 'dedicated patrons of Buddhism.' In Vietnam, daughters are 'flying ducks' who leave; sons carry the patriline. Mae khaao/mae chii (nuns) exist but occupy subordinate status. Transgender persons (katoey) face additional barriers. Being born female is interpreted as evidence of insufficient merit from past lives, yet women lead daily merit-making.
crossCuttingThemes.gameDesignImplication: Gender significantly shapes available choices and spiritual pathways. Female characters have different merit-earning strategies than males. Transgender characters face unique obstacles but also unique narrative opportunities (third-way monasticism). The tension between women's central practical role and subordinate institutional status creates rich gameplay dynamics.

crossCuttingThemes.description: All relationships have a senior-junior (pi-naung) dimension. Seniors must 'take care of' (liang) juniors; juniors can 'ask for' (yaun) from seniors. If you have something and someone asks, you must give or share. Generosity is socially enforced: claiming high status invites requests, creating a leveling mechanism. The more wealth you display, the more people ask. This extends to the merit-power nexus: wealthy patrons are simultaneously celebrated and beholden.
crossCuttingThemes.gameDesignImplication: Resource management mechanic where accumulating visible wealth triggers increasing social demands. Players must balance displaying status (which brings respect) with the cost of generosity (which depletes resources). The asking-for-things dynamic creates a natural tension between accumulation and redistribution.

crossCuttingThemes.description: Buddhism and spirit beliefs are not separate religions but interwoven aspects of one worldview. Healers use Buddhist scripture AND spirit power. Amulets, tattoos, and astrology coexist with merit-making. In Vietnam, the 'triple religion' (tam giao) + ancestor worship + animism creates a complex ecology. In Cambodia, Neak Ta guardian spirits have shrines within Buddhist pagodas. In Myanmar, nats are honored alongside the Buddha. Young Vietnamese combine Buddhism with tarot, astrology, and self-improvement. Animism forms the original substrate: 'rice is alive and the songs of birds bear messages.'
crossCuttingThemes.gameDesignImplication: The game world should present Buddhism, spirit beliefs, and other traditions as a seamless whole rather than separate systems. Players navigate multiple spiritual registers simultaneously -- making offerings to ancestors, seeking Buddhist merit, propitiating local spirits, and using protective amulets. Different countries weight these elements differently.

crossCuttingThemes.theme: Death and the Afterlife as Ongoing Obligation
crossCuttingThemes.description: Death is not an endpoint but a transition requiring sustained attention from the living. The dead need merit transfers, proper funeral rites, annual ceremonies (kotsaa, bangsokol, phrasat), and ongoing offerings. Violent or sudden deaths create dangerous spirits requiring special rituals. The deceased may appear in dreams, cause illness, or disturb the household until properly settled. In Laos, the phrasat ceremony may take years to afford but 'cannot be skipped.' The living and dead remain in active relationship.
crossCuttingThemes.gameDesignImplication: Death of NPCs (family members, community members) creates ongoing obligations and encounter opportunities. Players must manage relationships with the dead through rituals, offerings, and ceremonies. Neglecting the dead has concrete gameplay consequences (illness, bad luck, haunting). The type of death (good vs. dangerous) dramatically affects what the living must do.

crossCuttingThemes.description: Every wave of external influence (Indianization, Chinese civilization, Islam, colonialism, Christianity, modernity) undergoes localization in Southeast Asia. 'Though the Indic influence was widespread, they never made Southeast Asia become Indian.' Buddhist-Christian encounters involve complex translation dynamics. Modernist Buddhism adapts ancient practice for urban youth. Forest ordination repurposes monastic robes for environmental activism. Facebook becomes a platform for dhamma teaching. Each country localizes Buddhism differently: Theravada in mainland SE Asia, Mahayana in Vietnam, with unique syncretic layers everywhere.
crossCuttingThemes.gameDesignImplication: Country selection should meaningfully change gameplay -- not just aesthetics but fundamental mechanics. Vietnamese gameplay involves Mahayana/Confucian/Daoist elements absent from Thailand. Myanmar gameplay involves political-religious tensions unique to that context. The game should model how external influences (modernization, social media, globalization) create new choices within traditional frameworks.

crossCuttingThemes.description: Cross-religious encounters are fundamentally about translation: finding 'points of contact' while maintaining meaningful difference. In Myanmar, Christian evangelists use Buddhist vocabulary (dukkha, kutho, neikban) to communicate, but too much 'domestication' makes Christianity seem 'the same' (tu tu), while too much 'foreignization' makes it incomprehensible. The state regulates religious language by banning non-Buddhists from using ~100 Pali terms. Religious identity is increasingly contested as Buddhism intersects with nationalism, minority rights, and globalization.
crossCuttingThemes.gameDesignImplication: Include encounters where the player must navigate interfaith situations with real stakes. Word choice matters -- using the right religious vocabulary can open or close doors. The tension between religious tolerance and boundary-maintenance creates nuanced gameplay. In Myanmar, this becomes politically charged; in Vietnam, syncretism is the norm.

crossCuttingThemes.description: Cambodian Buddhism has pioneered the extension of Buddhist ethics to environmental protection. Forest ordination (tying robes to trees) makes trees sacred monks. The Dhammayietra peace walks traverse damaged landscapes as healing practice. Buddhadasa Bhikkhu's teaching that 'preserving ecology means conserving dhamma as dhamma is our inner ecology' provides philosophical foundation. The Buddha's life is intimately connected to forests: born under a tree, enlightened under a tree, first sermon in a forest, died in a forest. In Cambodia, the forest (Prei) is both terrifying and sacred.
crossCuttingThemes.gameDesignImplication: Environmental encounters where Buddhist ethics intersect with development pressures. Players can participate in tree ordinations, peace walks, and forest conservation as forms of spiritual practice. The tension between livelihood (clearing land) and preservation (protecting sacred trees) creates morally complex choices specific to the Cambodian context.

crossCuttingThemes.description: Pre-colonial Southeast Asian power operated through mandala structures: concentric circles of authority radiating from a center, attenuating with distance. Kings were devaraja (god-kings) whose power was religious as well as political. Succession was contested; loyalty negotiated through patronage, not fixed by territory. This persists in modern forms: the merit-power nexus in Myanmar, strongman politics in Cambodia, and patron-client relationships everywhere. Religious patronage (sponsoring temples, assemblies, ordinations) is simultaneously a spiritual and political act.
crossCuttingThemes.gameDesignImplication: The player exists within concentric circles of political/religious authority. Loyalty decisions (which patron to support, which monastery to affiliate with) have both spiritual and political consequences. Power is negotiated through generosity and merit-making, not just through force or wealth. This creates a political layer that integrates with the merit economy.

crossCuttingThemes.description: Across Southeast Asia, young people are renegotiating their relationship with Buddhism. In Vietnam, Buddhist temples offer retreats, meditation, celebrity talks, and social networking. Youth combine Buddhism with tarot, astrology, and psychology. 'Dharma illiteracy' is a recognized problem. In Myanmar, charismatic monks warn youth about forgetting culture. In Thailand, transgender youth seek alternative monastic spaces. Youth participation fluctuates: high during university, declining with careers, returning during existential crises. Social media (Facebook, streaming) becomes a primary channel for dhamma.
crossCuttingThemes.gameDesignImplication: The adolescence and young adulthood life stages should feature encounters about navigating modernity: choosing between traditional devotion and modernist philosophy, combining Buddhist practice with popular spirituality, using social media for dhamma, and managing the tension between career ambitions and spiritual obligations. Youth characters face different choices than their grandparents.
